   =20
      Emergent narrow banking
   =20
 =20

We have   talked a few   times   recently about, like,   the theory of bank=
ing. That theory goes roughly like this. Banks fund themselves with deposit=
s, which are basically=C2=A0short term=C2=A0and=C2=A0safe:=C2=A0If you have=
 $100 in a bank account, you expect that it will always be worth $100, and =
you expect to be able to withdraw five $20 bills any time you want. Meanwhi=
le banks invest their money in assets (loans, bonds) which are basically=C2=
=A0long term=C2=A0and=C2=A0risky: Banks make loans to risky businesses that=
 don=E2=80=99t have to be repaid for years.=C2=A0
This business has well-known problems. The main problems are:
=09The deposits are short-term but the assets are long-term: If all the dep=
ositors want their money back at once, it isn=E2=80=99t there, because it h=
as been loaned out. This can lead to bank runs, panics, fire sales of asset=
s,=C2=A0It=E2=80=99s a Wonderful Life, etc.=09The deposits are safe but the=
 assets are risky: If the bank makes bad loans and loses money, there won=
=E2=80=99t be enough money to pay back depositors, and bank deposits are me=
ant to be money, so depositors really count on their bank deposits being sa=
fe.

This business =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cfractional reserve banking,=E2=80=9D =
it is often called
  [1]
=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0is inherently risky and fragile. Everyone knows this, =
and there are standard methods to mitigate the risks. Banks have capital re=
quirements (they are partly funded with equity, not deposits, so if the ass=
ets lose value the depositors still get their money back). They have liquid=
ity requirements (some of their assets are short term and safe, so if depos=
itors want money back there=E2=80=99s some money to give them). There is sa=
fety-and-soundness regulation and supervision (the government tries to prev=
ent banks from making loans that will lose money). There is the lender of l=
ast resort (the central bank lends money to solvent banks that need cash, s=
o if depositors want their money back the banks can get it).=C2=A0There is =
deposit insurance (the government promises that depositors will get their m=
oney back, making bank accounts safer and runs less likely). Still there is=
 some unavoidable residue of fragility: The mismatch between the banks=E2=
=80=99 safe short term liabilities and their risky long term assets creates=
 risk, and somebody =E2=80=94=C2=A0if not depositors then the government =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0has to bear that risk.
You might draw one of two conclusions from this:
=09Oh well! Banking is=C2=A0good; there is social value in this fragility; =
it allows society to pool its safe capital to be able to take on risks. =E2=
=80=9CBanking is a way for people collectively to make long-term, risky bet=
s without noticing them, a way to pool risks so that everyone is safer and =
better-off,=E2=80=9D   I wrote last month. =E2=80=9CFinancial systems help =
us overcome a collective action problem,=E2=80=9D Steve Randy Waldman  wrot=
e in 2011:=C2=A0=E2=80=9CIn a world of investment projects whose costs and =
risks are perfectly transparent, most individuals would be frightened. =E2=
=80=A6=C2=A0A banking system is a superposition of fraud and genius that in=
terposes itself between investors and entrepreneurs=E2=80=9D to encourage i=
nvestment and make everyone better off.=09No, this is bad, it=E2=80=99s ant=
iquated, we should fix it. Let=E2=80=99s get rid of fractional reserve bank=
ing and do something else.

Conceptually the main way to do that goes something like this:
=09Banks should take short-term safe deposits and invest them in short-term=
 safe assets.=C2=A0=09Long-term risky assets should be funded with long-ter=
m risky liabilities.=C2=A0

This is loosely speaking called the =E2=80=9CChicago plan,=E2=80=9D or =E2=
=80=9Cnarrow banking.=E2=80=9D Banks would just hold cash for their deposit=
ors, so the cash would always be there and banks would not have any risk of=
 runs or credit losses. In modern banking, the banks would not hold =E2=80=
=9Ccash=E2=80=9D in the sense of $20 bills in a vault; rather, they=E2=80=
=99d take deposits and turn around and deposit the money as reserves at the=
 Federal Reserve.
  [2]
=C2=A0The Fed pays interest on reserves, so the banks could earn enough mon=
ey to pay for salaries and branches and still pay some interest to their cu=
stomers.=C2=A0(Though it=E2=80=99s not clear why a narrow bank would=C2=A0n=
eed=C2=A0branches
  [3]
; it would be a lot cheaper to operate a narrow bank than a full bank, so p=
resumably it would need less of an interest margin.)
Meanwhile, risky loans would come from people who=C2=A0intend=C2=A0to make =
risky loans: You could have, say, a loan fund that raises money from invest=
ors, locks up their money for 10 years, uses the money to make 10-year loan=
s, and then pays back the investors whatever the fund gets back on the loan=
s.
  [4]
=C2=A0If the fund makes bad loans, the investors lose money; they bear the =
risk knowingly and directly, unlike bank depositors who sort of don=E2=80=
=99t know where their money is going.
Perhaps this system would make borrowing more expensive, as the current sys=
tem of =E2=80=9Cfraud and genius=E2=80=9D disguises risks in the financial =
system and so makes it more willing to take risks cheaply. But perhaps it w=
ould be less fragile; it would cost society a little more most years but a =
lot less some years.
People sometimes talk about imposing this sort of system by legislation or =
regulation; this had a wave of popularity after 2008, though that has waned=
 in recent years. I have   written before that the Fed seems to have no int=
erest in narrow banking =E2=80=94=C2=A0it kind of hates it=C2=A0and has=C2=
=A0declined to approve a bank that tried to do exactly this model=C2=A0=E2=
=80=94=C2=A0and I don=E2=80=99t really expect fractional reserve banking to=
 end anytime soon.
But I do want to point out that there are indications that the markets are =
moving toward narrow banking all on their own. We   talked last month about=
 money market funds and the Fed=E2=80=99s reverse repo program.=C2=A0You ca=
n put your money in a money market fund that will just park it directly at =
the Fed, though when a money market fund parks cash at the Fed that is call=
ed =E2=80=9Creverse repo=E2=80=9D rather than =E2=80=9Creserves.=E2=80=9D A=
 money market fund that just parks its money at the Fed has essentially no =
risk: All of its assets are short-term (they are parked overnight) and safe=
 (its debtor is the Fed), so your money is safer there than it is at a bank=
. It has few expenses: It doesn=E2=80=99t need a branch network or a lot of=
=C2=A0lending officers to take in money online and park it at the Fed, so i=
t can pass on most of the interest that the Fed pays it directly to its cus=
tomers. And the Fed=C2=A0does=C2=A0pay it a lot of interest =E2=80=94=C2=A0=
reverse repos=C2=A0pay something like 4.8%=C2=A0=E2=80=94 so it can end up =
paying depositors more interest for taking less risk than a bank can.
In 2021, when interest rates were zero-ish everywhere and when there hadn=
=E2=80=99t been a big bank failure in a while, nobody had all that much inc=
entive to move their money from a bank account paying 0% interest to a mone=
y market fund paying barely more. But in 2023, when people were worried abo=
ut their banks=E2=80=99 safety anyway, moving from a bank account paying 0%=
 interest to a=C2=A0safer=C2=A0narrow money market fund paying 4.8% seems p=
retty attractive. And so people do.  The Wall Street Journal reports:

Main Street banks such as Citizens Financial Group Inc. and First Horizon C=
orp. said in recent first-quarter earnings reports they are having a toughe=
r time hanging onto customer money in a world where the Federal Reserve has=
 aggressively raised interest rates. To keep those depositors around, some =
lenders are paying more on savings accounts and turning to products like ce=
rtificates of deposit.
Though profits rose at many banks in the first quarter, the deposit decline=
s signal a fundamental change in their business. Deposits were plentiful in=
 the era of superlow rates because customers had little incentive to move t=
heir money elsewhere. Banks grew to rely on them as a cheap source of fundi=
ng that they could use to make loans or buy bonds and other securities. =C2=
=A0=E2=80=A6
In recent months, with the Fed continuing to raise rates, consumers and bus=
inesses at banks across the industry started moving their money from bank d=
eposits to higher-yield offerings such as money-market funds and Treasurys.=
 Customers who used to be satisfied keeping their money in accounts that pa=
id no interest decided they no longer were.=C2=A0
The collapse last month of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank accelerat=
ed the shift at some firms. Both banks were felled by panicky customers pul=
ling out their money after concerns started spreading about the banks=E2=80=
=99 health. Customers at those banks and some other smaller firms across th=
e U.S. started moving their money to the largest banks, betting they were s=
afer even in crisis. =E2=80=A6
Even the biggest banks, which enjoyed a windfall of deposits from smaller-b=
ank customers, had to pay more for deposits.
JPMorgan Chase & Co. said last week it picked up about $50 billion in new d=
eposits following March=E2=80=99s bank failures, but overall deposits were =
up only $37 billion from the end of 2022, indicating they would have droppe=
d if not for the industry turmoil. JPMorgan executives cautioned that the n=
ew deposits might not stick.

Then there is the other side of the Chicago plan: If banks don=E2=80=99t do=
 lending, lending needs to come from funds whose investors lock up their mo=
ney and consciously take on the risk of making loans. Those funds might hav=
e to=C2=A0charge=C2=A0more for loans than banks do, because their investors=
, unlike bank depositors,=C2=A0are knowingly locking up their money and tak=
ing risks and so want to be compensated.
And of course there is a lot of that in modern finance, loans that come fro=
m funds rather than from banks. Leveraged buyouts, for instance, used to be=
 financed with a combination of junk bonds (bought by funds, insurers, etc.=
) and bank loans (bought by banks). In recent years, the =E2=80=9Cbank loan=
s=E2=80=9D have increasingly been owned by hedge funds, collateralized loan=
 obligations, etc.; banks syndicate them but increasingly they end up on so=
meone else=E2=80=99s balance sheet. And more recently a lot of   leveraged =
buyouts have increasingly been financed   by private credit, deals with loa=
n funds (often sponsored by private equity firms) that cut out banks entire=
ly.
It is not just leveraged buyouts, though. Apollo Global Management Inc.   b=
ought a lot of Credit Suisse Group AG=E2=80=99s structured finance and weir=
d lending businesses, before Credit Suisse disappeared, forming a new lendi=
ng business funded by investors and its insurance company. Last month it ga=
ve PacWest Bancorp a $1.4 billion lending facility, and   I wrote: =E2=80=
=9CIt used to be that banks funded themselves with deposits and used the mo=
ney to make loans to private equity firms. Now banks fund themselves with l=
oans from private equity firms.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
Or here is   a Bloomberg News story today about Blackstone Inc.=E2=80=99s e=
arnings:

[Blackstone President Jon]=C2=A0Gray said that the tumult following the col=
lapse of three US regional lenders last month has created investment opport=
unities =E2=80=94 even after Blackstone backed a firm=E2=80=99s losing bid =
for Silicon Valley Bank. Blackstone has been talking to small banks about s=
tepping in to lend alongside them as more look to slim down their balance s=
heets.
=E2=80=9CBecause of the focus on liquidity they may want to find partners,=
=E2=80=9D Gray said.

Small banks don=E2=80=99t want to make loans =E2=80=9Cbecause of the focus =
on liquidity=E2=80=9D: Their deposits are more risky, so their banking need=
s to be narrower. So they turn to funds run by Blackstone, which raises lon=
g-term money from investors who want to take credit risk, to make their loa=
ns.
Basically in 2023 the market has created a lot of money-market funds that l=
ook like the deposit-taking side of narrow banking, and a lot of credit fun=
ds that look like the lending side of narrow banking.=C2=A0I don=E2=80=99t =
want to overstate this,=C2=A0and there are still a lot of big universal ban=
ks that are attracting deposits and making lots of loans. But they have som=
e competition from narrower banking.
One very speculative thing that I might say here is that the magic =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0the opacity, the =E2=80=9Csuperposition of fraud and genius=E2=80=
=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0of traditional fractional reserve banking is maybe a bit=
 harder to do in a world that=C2=A0pays attention to it. It is relatively e=
asy to find a bank=E2=80=99s balance sheet online, and if one person reads =
that balance sheet and notices that the bank is insolvent, it is relatively=
 easy for her to tweet that and have it go viral and cause a bank run.
  [5]
=C2=A0=E2=80=9CGame=E2=80=99s the same, just got more fierce,=E2=80=9D the =
vice chairman of the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp.   said last week, abou=
t bank runs in the age of social media. If banking relies on a certain opac=
ity, modern communications technology and social media might just make that=
 opacity harder to achieve.
  [6]
 If it=E2=80=99s too easy to see what the magician is doing, the magic does=
n=E2=80=99t work anymore.

   =20
      Bloodbath
   =20
 =20

The situation seems to be that it is mathematically impossible for Bed Bath=
 & Beyond Inc. to raise enough money by selling stock to avert bankruptcy. =
It=E2=80=99s not=C2=A0quite=C2=A0as simple as that =E2=80=94=C2=A0you can w=
rite down some scenarios where it=C2=A0can=C2=A0raise enough money =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0but they are pretty far-fetched.=C2=A0The Wall Street Journal repo=
rts:

Bed Bath & Beyond Inc. is preparing a bankruptcy filing for as early as thi=
s weekend as its falling stock price makes it near impossible to raise enou=
gh capital to avert default, according to people familiar with the matter.
The embattled retailer recently said it needed to raise $300 million from s=
hare sales by April 26 to stay out of chapter 11. =E2=80=A6
As of April 10, Bed Bath & Beyond had raised $48.5 million from its latest =
stock-sale effort. At the time, it had 178 million shares available to sell=
, which would only net the company about $70 million or $80 million given t=
he stock=E2=80=99s recent trading prices.=C2=A0
The retailer has warned that if it isn=E2=80=99t able to raise capital thro=
ugh its equity offering, it would have to file for bankruptcy and likely li=
quidate its assets. Bloomberg reported earlier Wednesday that Bed Bath & Be=
yond had resumed preparing for bankruptcy.

We  talked about this yesterday; as far as I can tell that Bloomberg report=
 led to a=C2=A0rally=C2=A0in the stock price, because I guess retail meme-s=
tock investors enjoy bankruptcy. Not=C2=A0enough=C2=A0of a rally, though, a=
nd  the stock is back down today.
If you are Bed Bath & Beyond, what do you do?=C2=A0Are you still selling st=
ock?=C2=A0Presumably you have been selling stock for two reasons:
=09Some desperate hope of raising enough to stay out of bankruptcy, and=09I=
f you fail, at least you raise some money to hand over to creditors, and at=
 this point you=E2=80=99re basically working for the creditors anyway.

As your hopes of avoiding bankruptcy fade, it becomes crueler and crueler t=
o your retail investors to keep selling them stock: They are just throwing =
away their money, and you know it. (And you have=C2=A0told=C2=A0them that =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0the prospectus for this stock offering is full of warnings t=
hat Bed Bath is probably going bankrupt =E2=80=94=C2=A0though they don=E2=
=80=99t seem to believe you.) On the other hand, as your hopes of avoiding =
bankruptcy fade, your fiduciary responsibilities are increasingly to your c=
reditors rather than your shareholders, and presumably those creditors want=
 you to raise as much money as you can from shareholders to hand over to th=
em. I can=E2=80=99t tell you how much I am looking forward to the lawsuits =
here.

 =20
   =20
      Bond sales
   =20
 =20

We   talked yesterday about a self-limiting feature of the recent US bankin=
g crisis: Interest rates went up, which caused banks to lose money on their=
 bond portfolios, which caused some bank failures, which caused interest ra=
tes =E2=80=94=C2=A0at least, the rates on the long-term bonds those banks h=
eld =E2=80=94 to go down. That doesn=E2=80=99t do much good for the banks t=
hat failed =E2=80=94=C2=A0they are already gone =E2=80=94=C2=A0but any bank=
 that muddled through now looks a bit less bad, because its bonds recovered=
 a bit. =E2=80=9CIf rising interest rates cause banks to fail,=E2=80=9D I w=
rote,=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cthat will lead to a recession, which will cause interes=
t rates to drop, which will rescue those banks. The trick is the timing.=E2=
=80=9D
For the banks that=C2=A0did=C2=A0fail, the beneficiary is whoever gets thei=
r bonds, which basically means the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. For a wh=
ile after the FDIC took over Silicon Valley Bank, there was some speculatio=
n that the FDIC would make a profit on the deal: It acquired SVB due to the=
 hole in its balance sheet caused by unrealized losses on its bond holdings=
, but then those bonds rallied after SVB failed, which in theory could have=
 repaired the hole in the balance sheet and left the FDIC with a profit. In=
 practice, probably not. The  Wall Street Journal reports:

The Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. has begun selling bonds it inherited fr=
om Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank to recoup the cost of rescuing th=
e failed banks=E2=80=99 depositors.
The FDIC put up for auction about $700 million of high-quality mortgage-bac=
ked bonds Tuesday in what could prove to be a test of how much the U.S. gov=
ernment recovers on the $114 billion in face value of the bonds it assumed.
=E2=80=9CPer the median price guidance from the six dealers who have publis=
hed price talk so far, the government should expect to get back around 86 c=
ents on the dollar for the entire portfolio,=E2=80=9D said Adam Murphy, fou=
nder of Empirasign, a bond-data service.
The FDIC estimates that its deposit-insurance fund will lose about $22.5 bi=
llion from depositor payouts. Most of that will be reimbursed through an as=
sessment on other banks, resulting in a $3.3 billion net loss, the agency s=
aid.=C2=A0

Long-term interest rates have come down a bit since SVB failed, as the bank=
 failures and general banking fragility has increased the risk of recession=
. On the other hand, the FDIC takeover does not totally avoid the basic dyn=
amics of a bank run, which are that if you have to pay out billions of doll=
ars of deposits by selling all your bonds at once, that will drive down the=
 prices of those bonds:

The market for mortgage-backed bonds weakened slightly this week, possibly =
in anticipation of future sales by the government. The risk premium investo=
rs demand to buy frequently traded 30-year mortgage bonds has roughly doubl=
ed since mid-February to about 0.65 percentage point over U.S. Treasury bon=
ds, according to FactSet.
=E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s going to be a challenge to absorb $90 billion of mark=
et value,=E2=80=9D said Walt Schmidt, a senior vice president at FHN Financ=
ial, a mortgage-bond brokerage.


 =20
   =20
      Chainsmokers
   =20
 =20

I guess the main difference between Wall Street and Silicon Valley is that =
if you are a senior executive at Goldman Sachs Group Inc. and also an elect=
ronic dance music DJ, your colleagues and mentors on Wall Street  will say =
things like =E2=80=9Cplease stop being a DJ=E2=80=9D or=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cwow t=
his is some textbook midlife-crisis stuff,=E2=80=9D whereas if you are a da=
nce-pop duo and also want to become venture capitalists, other venture capi=
talists will say things like =E2=80=9Cgreat idea,=E2=80=9D or=C2=A0=E2=80=
=9Cyou know, you guys are founders, too, and you built an amazing company,=
=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Ccan I be in your music video?=E2=80=9D Here is  a Blo=
omberg Businessweek feature on the founders of Mantis VC, a venture capital=
 firm, who are also the Chainsmokers, a dance-pop firm:=C2=A0

The Chainsmokers have given their haters something else to hold against the=
m: They=E2=80=99ve become venture capitalists, starting Mantis VC, a firm t=
hat raised a total of $110 million in the boom years of 2020 and 2021 from =
investors like TPG Inc. co-founder Jim Coulter and billionaire Mark Cuban. =
Cuban, in fact, can be seen in one of the duo=E2=80=99s TikTok videos, in w=
hich Pall rips the T-shirt off Taggart=E2=80=99s back during a performance =
and hands it to their benefactor to use as a napkin. =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99ve =
known them for a while,=E2=80=9D Cuban said in an email. =E2=80=9CLike them=
.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=A6
The Chainsmokers befriended the likes of Drew Houston, Dropbox Inc.=E2=80=
=99s chief executive officer, and Michael Seibel, a Y Combinator Inc. manag=
ing director, who urged them to chase their venture capitalist dreams. =E2=
=80=9CGuys like this kind of encouraged us,=E2=80=9D Pall said in an interv=
iew at the pair=E2=80=99s Hollywood Hills headquarters on a Tuesday afterno=
on in late July. =E2=80=A6
Pall recalled the early conversations with some of their friends in the hig=
hest echelons of the tech industry. =E2=80=9CThey were like, =E2=80=98You k=
now, you guys are founders, too, and you built an amazing company,=E2=80=99=
=E2=80=89=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=80=9C=E2=80=98There=E2=80=99s a lot of valu=
e in that that you can provide.=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D

I am somewhat forcing the comparison between the Chainsmokers and Goldman C=
EO David Solomon, who is also a DJ. For one thing, Solomon was a successful=
 investment banker long before he became a prominent (?) DJ. Nobody came up=
 to him after a DJ gig to be like =E2=80=9Cyou have built a successful play=
list, you could probably=C2=A0add a lot of value as an investment banker.=
=E2=80=9D Whereas with the Chainsmokers it was like =E2=80=9Cyou have some =
hit songs, good enough, welcome to venture capital.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0

       =20
  [1] The name comes from the classic textbook description in which banks t=
ake $1 of deposits, have to keep $X of cash reserves, and so can lend out t=
he other $(1 - X). X is some fraction that is considerably less than one (t=
hus =E2=80=9Cfractional reserve banking=E2=80=9D), and regulators set the r=
eserve requirements; higher reserve requirements lead to safer banking and =
less lending, while lower reserve requirements lead to riskier banking and =
more lending. This does not really describe how modern banking works, and i=
n fact the US  eliminated reserve requirements in 2020, though liquidity re=
gulation has some related effects.


  [2] What would the Fed do with it? Currently  the Fed=E2=80=99s balance s=
heet consists mostly of Treasury bonds and agency mortgage-backed securitie=
s. In a world where everybody=E2=80=99s checking accounts were effectively =
parked at the Fed, that balance sheet would expand. You could imagine the F=
ed using that expanded balance sheet to buy more Treasuries and mortgages, =
in which case narrow banking would be a somewhat more indirect form of trad=
itional mortgage banking, where banks borrow short-term from depositors to =
lend long-term to homeowners. (The Fed does not bear the *credit* risk of t=
hese mortgages, which are guaranteed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, but the=
n Fannie and Freddie are government entities as well; in practice the credi=
t risk is owned either by the US Treasury or by investors who consciously b=
uy risk-transfer securities from Fannie and Freddie.) I guess you could ima=
gine some other approach, though. The Fed could use its expanded balance sh=
eet to, like, buy corporate bonds, or to invest in the funds that make risk=
y loans; you could have a system that is a lot like fractional reserve bank=
ing but intermediated by the Fed.


  [3] Or equity capital for that matter, or a return on equity, or highly p=
aid executives, etc.


  [4] You don=E2=80=99t need exactly these features. =E2=80=9CA loan fund t=
hat raises money from equity investors and offers them daily liquidity at n=
et asset value, possibly with some gates to limit withdrawals, and then lev=
erages that money by borrowing long-term from debt investors=E2=80=9D is pr=
obably good enough; the point is for all of the investors to knowingly bear=
 risk. =E2=80=9CA loan fund that raises money from equity investors and the=
n borrows overnight from big banks=E2=80=9D is maybe *not* good enough: Ove=
rnight borrowing is too close to being bank deposits, too short-term and sa=
fe-seeming. (Morgan Ricks addresses the fragility of short-term borrowing i=
n his book =E2=80=9C The Money Problem.=E2=80=9D)


  [5] Or write a Substack post.  One version of the Silicon Valley Bank sto=
ry is that Byrne Hobart read SVB=E2=80=99s balance sheet and  wrote a newsl=
etter in February saying =E2=80=9Con a mark-to-market basis, they were brok=
e last quarter, albeit still liquid.=E2=80=9D And then his venture capitali=
st readers texted all their portfolio companies telling them to get their m=
oney out of SVB, and they did, and SVB was no longer liquid.


  [6] On the other hand, that was kind of the promise of crypto: Bitcoin wa=
s created in part as a reaction to the financial crisis of 2008, and early =
crypto enthusiasts were very skeptical of fractional reserve banking. There=
 was an idea that by making finance transparent and public on the blockchai=
n, crypto would   create a new system that would be free from the risk and =
fragility of fractional reserve banking. And then crypto built an opaque le=
veraged fractional reserve system anyway, and it blew up just like 2008. =
=E2=80=9CThere is some =E2=80=A6 natural =E2=80=A6 human =E2=80=A6 longing =
=E2=80=A6 to borrow short and lend long with no equity,=E2=80=9D   I once w=
rote, about the collapses of crypto shadow banks.

none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  the theory of banking<=
/a>. That theory goes roughly like this. Banks fund themselves with deposit=
s, which are basically=C2=A0<em>short term=C2=A0</em>and=C2=A0<em>safe:=C2=
=A0</em>If you have $100 in a bank account, you expect that it will always =
be worth $100, and you expect to be able to withdraw five $20 bills any tim=
e you want. Meanwhile banks invest their money in assets (loans, bonds) whi=
ch are basically=C2=A0<em>long term=C2=A0</em>and=C2=A0<em>risky</em>: Bank=
s make loans to risky businesses that don=E2=80=99t have to be repaid for y=
ears.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This business has well-known problems. The mai=
n problems are:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The deposits are short-term but the as=
sets are long-term: If all the depositors want their money back at once, it=
 isn=E2=80=99t there, because it has been loaned out. This can lead to bank=
 runs, panics, fire sales of assets,=C2=A0<em>It=E2=80=99s a Wonderful Life=
</em>, etc.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The deposits are safe =
but the assets are risky: If the bank makes bad loans and loses money, ther=
e won=E2=80=99t be enough money to pay back depositors, and bank deposits a=
re meant to be <em>money</em>, so depositors really count on their bank dep=
osits being safe.</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This business =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cfraction=
al reserve banking,=E2=80=9D it is often called<a class=3D"footnote-index" =
href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !import=
ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: =
inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0is inherently risky and fragile. Everyone knows th=
is, and there are standard methods to mitigate the risks. Banks have capita=
l requirements (they are partly funded with equity, not deposits, so if the=
 assets lose value the depositors still get their money back). They have li=
quidity requirements (some of their assets are short term and safe, so if d=
epositors want money back there=E2=80=99s some money to give them). There i=
s safety-and-soundness regulation and supervision (the government tries to =
prevent banks from making loans that will lose money). There is the lender =
of last resort (the central bank lends money to solvent banks that need cas=
h, so if depositors want their money back the banks can get it).=C2=A0There=
 is deposit insurance (the government promises that depositors will get the=
ir money back, making bank accounts safer and runs less likely). Still ther=
e is some unavoidable residue of fragility: The mismatch between the banks=
=E2=80=99 safe short term liabilities and their risky long term assets crea=
tes risk, and somebody =E2=80=94=C2=A0if not depositors then the government=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0has to bear that risk.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You might draw one of two conclusions from thi=
s:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Oh well! Banking is=C2=A0<em>good</em=
>; there is social value in this fragility; it allows society to pool its s=
afe capital to be able to take on risks. =E2=80=9CBanking is a way for peop=
le collectively to make long-term, risky bets without noticing them, a way =
to pool risks so that everyone is safer and better-off,=E2=80=9D <a href=3D=
"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31230764.264442/aHR0cHM6Ly93=
d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDMtMTcvYmlnLWJhbmtzLXR=
ydXN0LWZpcnN0LXJlcHVibGljLXdpdGgtdGhlaXItbW9uZXk_Y21waWQ9QkJEMDQyMDIzX01PTk=
VZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09M=
jMwNDIwJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cC46c60efc" =
itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"c=
olor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; =
border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color:=
 #ccc;">  I wrote last month</a>. =E2=80=9CFinancial systems help us overco=
me a collective action problem,=E2=80=9D Steve Randy Waldman <a href=3D"htt=
ps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31230764.264442/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cu=
aW50ZXJmbHVpZGl0eS5jb20vdjIvMjY2OS5odG1s/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB12f121db"=
 itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"=
color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px;=
 border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color=
: #ccc;"> wrote in 2011</a>:=C2=A0=E2=80=9CIn a world of investment project=
s whose costs and risks are perfectly transparent, most individuals would b=
e frightened. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0A banking system is a superposition of fraud a=
nd genius that interposes itself between investors and entrepreneurs=E2=80=
=9D to encourage investment and make everyone better off.</li>=09<li style=
=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">No, this is bad, it=E2=80=99s antiquated, we shoul=
d fix it. Let=E2=80=99s get rid of fractional reserve banking and do someth=
ing else.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Conceptually the main way to do that goes some=
thing like this:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Banks should take short-term safe depo=
sits and invest them in short-term safe assets.=C2=A0</li>=09<li style=3D"m=
argin-bottom: 5px;">Long-term risky assets should be funded with long-term =
risky liabilities.=C2=A0</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is loosely speaking called the =E2=80=9C<=
a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31230764.264442/aHR=
0cHM6Ly9lbi53aWtpcGVkaWEub3JnL3dpa2kvQ2hpY2Fnb19wbGFu/607f07ceef4b8524a319a=
23cBb2bd5350" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bla=
nk" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-botto=
m-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; bac=
kground-color: #ccc;">Chicago plan</a>,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9Cnarrow banking=
.=E2=80=9D Banks would just hold cash for their depositors, so the cash wou=
ld always be there and banks would not have any risk of runs or credit loss=
es. In modern banking, the banks would not hold =E2=80=9Ccash=E2=80=9D in t=
he sense of $20 bills in a vault; rather, they=E2=80=99d take deposits and =
turn around and deposit the money as reserves at the Federal Reserve.<a cla=
ss=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-de=
coration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #=
000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent=
 !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>=C2=A0The Fed pays interest on reserves, so the banks could earn enough=
 money to pay for salaries and branches and still pay some interest to thei=
r customers.=C2=A0(Though it=E2=80=99s not clear why a narrow bank would=C2=
=A0<em>need=C2=A0</em>branches<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote=
-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-botto=
m-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !impo=
rtant; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a>; it would be a lot cheaper to operate a narrow bank than a full bank, =
so presumably it would need less of an interest margin.)</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Meanwhile, risky loans would come from people =
who=C2=A0<em>intend=C2=A0</em>to make risky loans: You could have, say, a l=
oan fund that raises money from investors, locks up their money for 10 year=
s, uses the money to make 10-year loans, and then pays back the investors w=
hatever the fund gets back on the loans.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D=
"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bo=
rder-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: =
none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-=
block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>=C2=A0If the fund makes bad loans, the investors lose money; they bear =
the risk knowingly and directly, unlike bank depositors who sort of don=E2=
=80=99t know where their money is going.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Perhaps this system would make borrowing more =
expensive, as the current system of =E2=80=9Cfraud and genius=E2=80=9D disg=
uises risks in the financial system and so makes it more willing to take ri=
sks cheaply. But perhaps it would be less fragile; it would cost society a =
little more most years but a lot less some years.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">People sometimes talk about imposing this sort=
 of system by legislation or regulation; this had a wave of popularity afte=
r 2008, though that has waned in recent years. I have <a href=3D"https://li=
nk.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31230764.264442/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21=
iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTktMDMtMDgvdGhlLWZlZC12ZXJzdXMtdGhlLW=
5hcnJvdy1iYW5rP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA0MjAyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmd=
XRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMDQyMCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlz=
dHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBb1f4ca0f" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  written before</a> tha=
t the Fed seems to have no interest in narrow banking =E2=80=94=C2=A0it kin=
d of hates it=C2=A0and has=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusin=
ess.com/click/31230764.264442/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL=
2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTgtMDktMDYvZmVkLXJlamVjdHMtYmFuay1mb3ItYmVpbmctdG9vLXNhZmU_=
Y21waWQ9QkJEMDQyMDIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5=
ld3NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwNDIwJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07cee=
f4b8524a319a23cB791ccc30" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" ta=
rget=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; =
border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style=
: solid; background-color: #ccc;">declined to approve</a> a bank that tried=
 to do exactly this model=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0and I don=E2=80=99t really ex=
pect fractional reserve banking to end anytime soon.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But I do want to point out that there are indi=
cations that the markets are moving toward narrow banking all on their own.=
 We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31230764.26444=
2/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDMtMzAvY=

; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-colo=
r: #ccc;">  talked last month about money market funds</a> and the Fed=E2=
=80=99s reverse repo program.=C2=A0You can put your money in a money market=
 fund that will just park it directly at the Fed, though when a money marke=
t fund parks cash at the Fed that is called =E2=80=9Creverse repo=E2=80=9D =
rather than =E2=80=9Creserves.=E2=80=9D A money market fund that just parks=
 its money at the Fed has essentially no risk: All of its assets are short-=
term (they are parked overnight) and safe (its debtor is the Fed), so your =
money is safer there than it is at a bank. It has few expenses: It doesn=E2=
=80=99t need a branch network or a lot of=C2=A0lending officers to take in =
money online and park it at the Fed, so it can pass on most of the interest=
 that the Fed pays it directly to its customers. And the Fed=C2=A0<em>does=

ef=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31230764.264442/aHR0cHM=
6Ly9mcmVkLnN0bG91aXNmZWQub3JnL3Nlcmllcy9SUlBPTlRTWUFXQVJE/607f07ceef4b8524a=

can end up paying depositors more interest for taking less risk than a bank=
 can.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In 2021, when interest rates were zero-ish eve=
rywhere and when there hadn=E2=80=99t been a big bank failure in a while, n=
obody had all that much incentive to move their money from a bank account p=
aying 0% interest to a money market fund paying barely more. But in 2023, w=
hen people were worried about their banks=E2=80=99 safety anyway, moving fr=
om a bank account paying 0% interest to a=C2=A0<em>safer=C2=A0</em>narrow m=
oney market fund paying 4.8% seems pretty attractive. And so people do. <a =
href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31230764.264442/aHR0c=
HM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy90aGUtZXJhLW9mLWVhc3ktZGVwb3NpdHMtaXMtb3Zl=

ant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-=
style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> The Wall Street Journal reports</a>=
:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Main Street banks such as Citizens Financial G=
roup Inc. and First Horizon Corp. said in recent first-quarter earnings rep=
orts they are having a tougher time hanging onto customer money in a world =
where the Federal Reserve has aggressively raised interest rates. To keep t=
hose depositors around, some lenders are paying more on savings accounts an=
d turning to products like certificates of deposit.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Though profits rose at many banks in the first=
 quarter, the deposit declines signal a fundamental change in their busines=
s. Deposits were plentiful in the era of superlow rates because customers h=
ad little incentive to move their money elsewhere. Banks grew to rely on th=
em as a cheap source of funding that they could use to make loans or buy bo=
nds and other securities. =C2=A0=E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In recent months, with the Fed continuing to r=
aise rates, consumers and businesses at banks across the industry started m=
oving their money from bank deposits to higher-yield offerings such as mone=
y-market funds and Treasurys. Customers who used to be satisfied keeping th=
eir money in accounts that paid no interest decided they no longer were.=C2=
=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The collapse last month of Silicon Valley Bank=
 and Signature Bank accelerated the shift at some firms. Both banks were fe=
lled by panicky customers pulling out their money after concerns started sp=
reading about the banks=E2=80=99 health. Customers at those banks and some =
other smaller firms across the U.S. started moving their money to the large=
st banks, betting they were safer even in crisis. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Even the biggest banks, which enjoyed a windfa=
ll of deposits from smaller-bank customers, had to pay more for deposits.</=
p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">JPMorgan Chase & Co. said last week it pic=
ked up about $50 billion in new deposits following March=E2=80=99s bank fai=
lures, but overall deposits were up only $37 billion from the end of 2022, =
indicating they would have dropped if not for the industry turmoil. JPMorga=
n executives cautioned that the new deposits might not stick.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Then there is the other side of the Chicago pl=

se investors lock up their money and consciously take on the risk of making=
 loans. Those funds might have to=C2=A0<em>charge=C2=A0</em>more for loans =
than banks do, because their investors, unlike bank depositors,=C2=A0are kn=
owingly locking up their money and taking risks and so want to be compensat=
ed.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And of course there is a lot of that in modern=
 finance, loans that come from funds rather than from banks. Leveraged buyo=
uts, for instance, used to be financed with a combination of junk bonds (bo=
ught by funds, insurers, etc.) and bank loans (bought by banks). In recent =
years, the =E2=80=9Cbank loans=E2=80=9D have increasingly been owned by hed=
ge funds, collateralized loan obligations, etc.; banks syndicate them but i=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Small banks don=E2=80=99t want to make loans =

credit risk, to make their loans.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Basically in 2023 the market has created a lot=

king, and a lot of credit funds that look like the lending side of narrow b=
anking.=C2=A0I don=E2=80=99t want to overstate this,=C2=A0and there are sti=
ll a lot of big universal banks that are attracting deposits and making lot=
s of loans. But they have some competition from narrower banking.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">One very speculative thing that I might say he=

rve banking is maybe a bit harder to do in a world that=C2=A0<em>pays atten=

=C2=A0<em>can=C2=A0</em>raise enough money =E2=80=94=C2=A0but they are pret=

=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0=
00000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-b=
ottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">=
Wall Street Journal reports</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bed Bath & Beyond Inc. is preparing a bank=
ruptcy filing for as early as this weekend as its falling stock price makes=
 it near impossible to raise enough capital to avert default, according to =
people familiar with the matter.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The embattled retailer recently said it needed=

11. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As of April 10, Bed Bath & Beyond had rais=
ed $48.5 million from its latest stock-sale effort. At the time, it had 178=
 million shares available to sell, which would only net the company about $=
70 million or $80 million given the stock=E2=80=99s recent trading prices.=
=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The retailer has warned that if it isn=E2=80=
=99t able to raise capital through its equity offering, it would have to fi=
le for bankruptcy and likely liquidate its assets. Bloomberg reported earli=
er Wednesday that Bed Bath & Beyond had resumed preparing for bankruptc=
y.</p>
</blockquote>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are Bed Bath & Beyond, what do you =
do?=C2=A0<em>Are you still selling stock?=C2=A0</em>Presumably you have bee=
n selling stock for two reasons:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Some desperate hope of raising enough=
 to stay out of bankruptcy, and</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">If=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As your hopes of avoiding bankruptcy fade, it =
becomes crueler and crueler to your retail investors to keep selling them s=

rder-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: =
solid; background-color: #ccc;">  talked yesterday</a> about a self-limitin=
g feature of the recent US banking crisis: Interest rates went up, which ca=
used banks to lose money on their bond portfolios, which caused some bank f=
ailures, which caused interest rates =E2=80=94=C2=A0at least, the rates on =
the long-term bonds those banks held =E2=80=94 to go down. That doesn=E2=80=
=99t do much good for the banks that failed =E2=80=94=C2=A0they are already=
 gone =E2=80=94=C2=A0but any bank that muddled through now looks a bit less=
 bad, because its bonds recovered a bit. =E2=80=9CIf rising interest rates =
cause banks to fail,=E2=80=9D I wrote,=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cthat will lead to a re=
cession, which will cause interest rates to drop, which will rescue those b=
anks. The trick is the timing.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">For the banks that=C2=A0<em>did=C2=A0</em>fail=
, the beneficiary is whoever gets their bonds, which basically means the Fe=
deral Deposit Insurance Corp. For a while after the FDIC took over Silicon =
Valley Bank, there was some speculation that the FDIC would make a profit o=
n the deal: It acquired SVB due to the hole in its balance sheet caused by =
unrealized losses on its bond holdings, but then those bonds rallied after =
SVB failed, which in theory could have repaired the hole in the balance she=
et and left the FDIC with a profit. In practice, probably not. The <a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/31230764.264442/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9hcnRpY2xlcy9mZGljLXN0YXJ0cy1zZWxsaW5nLTExNC1iaWxsaW9uLW9m=
LWJvbmRzLWZyb20tZmFpbGVkLWJhbmtzLTM3ZTJiMzZmP21vZD1pdHBfd3NqJnJ1PXlhaG9v/60=
7f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBdffd8271" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsc=
ope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> Wall Street Journal reports</a>:<=
/p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. has begun =
selling bonds it inherited from Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank to r=
ecoup the cost of rescuing the failed banks=E2=80=99 depositors.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The FDIC put up for auction about $700 million=
 of high-quality mortgage-backed bonds Tuesday in what could prove to be a =
test of how much the U.S. government recovers on the $114 billion in face v=
alue of the bonds it assumed.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CPer the median price guidance from th=
e six dealers who have published price talk so far, the government should e=
xpect to get back around 86 cents on the dollar for the entire portfolio,=
=E2=80=9D said Adam Murphy, founder of Empirasign, a bond-data service.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The FDIC estimates that its deposit-insurance =
fund will lose about $22.5 billion from depositor payouts. Most of that wil=
l be reimbursed through an assessment on other banks, resulting in a $3.3 b=
illion net loss, the agency said.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Long-term interest rates have come down a bit =
since SVB failed, as the bank failures and general banking fragility has in=
creased the risk of recession. On the other hand, the FDIC takeover does no=
t totally avoid the basic dynamics of a bank run, which are that if you hav=
e to pay out billions of dollars of deposits by selling all your bonds at o=
nce, that will drive down the prices of those bonds:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The market for mortgage-backed bonds weakened =
slightly this week, possibly in anticipation of future sales by the governm=
ent. The risk premium investors demand to buy frequently traded 30-year mor=
tgage bonds has roughly doubled since mid-February to about 0.65 percentage=
 point over U.S. Treasury bonds, according to FactSet.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s going to be a challenge =
to absorb $90 billion of market value,=E2=80=9D said Walt Schmidt, a senior=
 vice president at FHN Financial, a mortgage-bond brokerage.</p>
</blockquote>
